shortDescription: For resident individuals with income from salary/pension, one house property, other sources (interest, dividends), and agricultural income up to ₹5,000. Total income must not exceed ₹50 lakh.
longDescription: ITR-1 (Sahaj) is the simplest and most commonly used ITR form. It is designed for straightforward income profiles — primarily salaried employees and pensioners. The form has been designed to minimize data entry and is pre-filled with employer-reported data in many cases. For FY 2025-26, the primary changes are in the tax computation — the new tax regime default with the revised 87A rebate up to ₹12 lakh.
incomeLimitNote: Total income from all sources must not exceed ₹50,00,000
residencyNote: ONLY ordinary resident individuals can file ITR-1. RNOR (Resident but Not Ordinarily Resident) and NRIs must use ITR-2.

eligibilityCriteria.detail: Must be ordinarily resident in India — satisfies both conditions u/s 6(1) and 6(6). RNOR and NRIs are excluded.
Salary from one or more employers. Pension income from former employer. Family pension is treated as 'other sources' — standard deduction u/s 57 applies.

eligibilityCriteria.criterion: Income from one house property only
eligibilityCriteria.detail: Self-occupied, let-out, or deemed let-out — but ONLY ONE property. If more than one house property exists, must use ITR-2.

eligibilityCriteria.criterion: Income from other sources (interest, dividends, family pension, etc.)
eligibilityCriteria.detail: Savings bank interest, fixed deposit interest, dividends from shares/MFs, NSC accrued interest, etc. Does NOT include winnings from lotteries, horse racing, or card games (those require ITR-2).
Small agricultural income up to ₹5,000 is permitted in ITR-1. Agricultural income above ₹5,000 requires ITR-2.

exclusionCriteria.detail: NRIs and RNORs cannot use ITR-1 regardless of the nature of their income.
Any person who is or was a director in a company at any time during FY 2025-26. Includes nominee directors.

exclusionCriteria.criterion: Held unlisted equity shares at any time during FY 2025-26
exclusionCriteria.detail: Even holding one share in an unlisted company for one day disqualifies ITR-1.
Any capital gain — including LTCG u/s 112A even within the ₹1.25L exempt limit — disqualifies ITR-1. Capital gains require ITR-2.
Ownership of two or more house properties (even if one is under construction, or both self-occupied).

exclusionCriteria.criterion: Foreign assets or signing authority in foreign account
exclusionCriteria.detail: Any foreign bank account, foreign investment, ESOP of foreign company, or signing authority in any account abroad.
Any business or professional income — including freelance or part-time consultancy — disqualifies ITR-1.
exclusionCriteria.consequence: Must use ITR-3 (regular books) or ITR-4 (presumptive)

exclusionCriteria.detail: If total income from all sources exceeds ₹50,00,000.

exclusionCriteria.criterion: Income from winnings (lottery, horse racing, online gaming)
exclusionCriteria.detail: Winnings from lotteries, crossword puzzles, horse racing, card games, online gaming are taxed at special rates and require ITR-2.
HUFs cannot file ITR-1. They must use ITR-2 (minimum) regardless of income type.
exclusionCriteria.consequence: Must use ITR-2 (or ITR-3/ITR-4 if business income)

hufNote: HUFs are completely excluded from ITR-1. Even a HUF with only salary equivalent income must use ITR-2.

incomeHeadsAllowed.head: Income from Salary / Pension
incomeHeadsAllowed.details: Includes basic salary, DA, HRA, special allowances, perquisites, profit in lieu of salary. Exemptions u/s 10 (HRA, LTA, gratuity, etc.) are deducted before reporting.

incomeHeadsAllowed.head: Income from House Property
incomeHeadsAllowed.details: One house property only. Self-occupied: deduction of up to ₹2 lakh interest on home loan u/s 24(b) under old regime. Let-out: NAV computation and 30% standard deduction.

incomeHeadsAllowed.head: Income from Other Sources
incomeHeadsAllowed.details: Savings bank interest, fixed deposit interest, dividends, NSC interest, RD/TD interest, family pension, casual income (not lottery/gambling). Deduction u/s 80TTA (₹10,000 savings interest) available under old regime.

commonMisconceptions: Misconception: LTCG u/s 112A is exempt up to ₹1.25L so ITR-1 can be filed. Fact: ANY capital gain income — even fully exempt — requires ITR-2. Capital gains must be disclosed regardless.
Misconception: A salaried director of a small company can use ITR-1. Fact: Being a director in ANY company (even a startup or family company) disqualifies ITR-1. Must use ITR-2.

Misconception: If someone holds ESOP shares of a foreign company and they're unvested, ITR-1 can be used. Fact: Any beneficial interest in unlisted shares (including unvested ESOPs) disqualifies ITR-1.
Misconception: ITR-1 can be used if interest income is very small. Fact: The type of income matters, not the amount. Even ₹100 capital gain requires ITR-2.
Misconception: If freelance income was incidental/one-time, ITR-1 can be used. Fact: ANY professional or business income — even a single consultancy payment — requires ITR-3 or ITR-4.

fy202526Changes.summary: New tax regime is the DEFAULT for FY 2025-26
fy202526Changes.detail: Taxpayers who want old regime deductions (80C, 80D, HRA, etc.) must explicitly opt for old regime when filing ITR

fy202526Changes.summary: Zero tax up to ₹12,75,000 for salaried under new regime
fy202526Changes.detail: 87A rebate of ₹60,000 applicable if taxable income ≤ ₹12L. With standard deduction of ₹75,000, salaried individuals with salary ≤ ₹12.75L pay zero tax.

fy202526Changes.summary: Standard deduction continues at ₹75,000 under new regime
fy202526Changes.detail: Introduced in FY 2024-25, continues for FY 2025-26

dscNote: DSC is not mandatory for ITR-1 filers. Aadhaar OTP or net banking EVC is sufficient.
